name: ESG Opportunity Sprint
summary: A short, cross-functional sprint for finding practical sustainability wins in an existing asset. It focuses on operational improvements that reduce waste, improve comfort, or strengthen reporting without waiting for a major redevelopment.
whenToUseIt: Use this when an owner asks for a sustainability roadmap, when energy costs are climbing, when NABERS or LEED-style performance needs improvement, or when the team wants quick wins that can build momentum for larger ESG work.
steps: Choose one asset question - Frame the sprint around a clear target such as reducing after-hours energy use, improving waste segregation, improving comfort complaints, or building a stronger emissions baseline.
Pull a mixed working group - Include property management, facilities, engineering, relevant contractors, and where useful tenant representatives. ESG improvements often fail when they stay siloed.
Map waste, friction, and quick wins - Walk the building and the data. Look for wasted runtime, blind spots in metering, process duplication, poor signage, comfort issues, and capex-light upgrades.
Prioritize by impact and effort - Separate low-effort actions from larger projects. Immediate wins create credibility; longer investments need a clearer business case.
Convert the sprint into a tracked plan - Assign owners, dates, expected outcomes, and reporting points so the sprint ends in action rather than a slide deck.
example: A mixed-use asset runs a two-week ESG Opportunity Sprint focused on after-hours energy. The team finds cleaners triggering full-floor HVAC for small zones, poor shut-down routines, and missing submeter visibility. The resulting plan includes scheduling changes, signage, contractor instructions, and a targeted metering upgrade case.
